title: 2026 IDSA Clinical Practice Guidelines on Prevention of Invasive Aspergillosis in Adult Solid Organ Transplant Recipients – Liver, Kidney and Pancreas Transplant Recipients
one_line_summary: IDSA guideline suggests against universal anti-Aspergillus prophylaxis in liver, kidney, and pancreas transplant recipients, recommending targeted prophylaxis with echinocandins or newer triazoles only for high-risk liver transplant patients.
key_pearls: In liver transplant recipients, **universal anti-Aspergillus prophylaxis is not recommended** (conditional, low certainty) due to lack of clear benefit and potential for adverse events.

**Targeted anti-Aspergillus prophylaxis** is suggested for liver transplant recipients at high risk for IA, defined by **peri-transplant RRT, re-transplantation, or fulminant hepatic failure** (conditional, very low certainty).

For liver transplant recipients requiring prophylaxis, preferred agents are an **echinocandin** or a **newer mold-active triazole (voriconazole, posaconazole, or isavuconazole)** rather than amphotericin B formulations or itraconazole.
The pooled incidence of IA in liver transplant recipients not receiving anti-Aspergillus prophylaxis is **1.9%**, but rises to **16.9%** in high-risk groups, supporting a targeted approach.
In kidney and pancreas transplant recipients, the pooled incidence of IA is very low (**0.8%** and **0.8%**, respectively), and universal anti-Aspergillus prophylaxis is not supported.

Echinocandins offer advantages of **minimal drug-drug interactions** and activity against *Candida* spp., while mold-active triazoles provide broader coverage but require **careful monitoring of immunosuppressant levels**.
The optimal duration of targeted prophylaxis is typically **until hospital discharge or up to 28 days post-transplant**.

recommendation_blocks.topic: Liver Transplant Recipients: Optimal Anti-Aspergillus Prophylaxis Strategy
recommendation_blocks.narrative:
- Universal prophylaxis is defined as administration to all liver transplant recipients.
- The systematic review identified **6 RCTs** (n=828) comparing universal anti-Aspergillus prophylaxis (anidulafungin, micafungin, itraconazole, liposomal amphotericin B) to fluconazole or no prophylaxis.
- The pooled incidence of IA in control groups was **1.9%** (95% CI 1.4-2.5%) from a mapping review of 40 studies (n=14,385).
- For universal vs. no prophylaxis, the risk difference (RD) for IA was **0.1%** (95% CI -0.8 to 0.2%; low CoE).
- Non-serious adverse events may be more frequent with universal prophylaxis (RD **6.7%**; 95% CI -0.5% to 18.1%; low CoE).
- For targeted vs. no prophylaxis in high-risk patients, 3 observational studies (n=145) showed a reduction in IA incidence with an RD of **-13.7%** (95% CI -15.9 to -5.8%; very low CoE).
- High-risk factors identified: **renal replacement therapy (RRT)** in the peri-transplant period, **re-transplantation**, and **transplantation for fulminant hepatic failure**.
- A sensitivity analysis suggests targeted prophylaxis is justified when the baseline IA incidence is **≥5%**.

recommendations.statement: In liver transplant recipients, we suggest against using universal anti-Aspergillus prophylaxis.
In liver transplant recipients, we suggest targeted anti-Aspergillus prophylaxis for individuals at high risk of invasive aspergillosis.

recommendation_blocks.topic: Liver Transplant Recipients: Optimal Choice of Prophylactic Agent
recommendation_blocks.narrative:
- Direct comparative evidence is limited; 2 network meta-analyses and 2 observational studies were reviewed.
- Echinocandins (micafungin, anidulafungin, caspofungin) show breakthrough IA rates of **0-3%** and favorable safety profiles with minimal drug-drug interactions.
- Newer mold-active triazoles (voriconazole, posaconazole, isavuconazole) have breakthrough IFI rates generally **<3%**. Voriconazole is the most studied; data for posaconazole and isavuconazole are extrapolated from other populations.
- Itraconazole is limited by **unreliable absorption** and **significant GI side effects** (non-serious AE in 24.3% of recipients).
- Amphotericin B formulations are associated with **nephrotoxicity** and infusion-related reactions; SAEs leading to discontinuation occurred in **5-13%** of patients.
- Echinocandins have no activity against *Cryptococcus* or *Rhizopus* spp.; triazoles require careful therapeutic drug monitoring of calcineurin inhibitors.

recommendations.statement: In liver transplant recipients requiring anti-Aspergillus prophylaxis, we suggest using an echinocandin or a newer anti-mold triazole (voriconazole, posaconazole or isavuconazole) rather than amphotericin B formulations or itraconazole.

recommendation_blocks.narrative:
- A mapping review of 34 observational studies (n=35,240) found a pooled IA incidence of **0.8%** (95% CI 0.6-1.2%).
- Two large US registry studies (n=90,608) reported a pooled IA incidence of **0.4%** (95% CI 0.2-0.6%).
- Risk factors for IA include prolonged pre-transplant RRT, older age, preceding bacterial pneumonia, diabetes, and leukopenia.
- Given the very low incidence, the balance of benefits and harms does not favor universal prophylaxis.

recommendations.statement: The current available evidence does not support the use of universal IA prophylaxis in kidney transplant recipients.

recommendation_blocks.narrative:
- A mapping review of 11 studies (n=1,234) found a pooled IA incidence of **0.8%** (95% CI 0.3-1.7%).
- The one-year all-cause **mortality** among IA cases was **70%**, though only 3 deaths were directly attributable to IA.
- IA onset ranged from **90 to 340 days** post-transplant, predominantly involving lungs and sinuses.
- The low event rate and poor-quality evidence preclude robust risk factor analysis.

recommendations.statement: The current available evidence does not support the use of universal IA prophylaxis in pancreas transplant recipients.

bedside_protocol.title: Risk Stratify Liver Transplant Recipients
bedside_protocol.action: Identify patients at high risk for invasive aspergillosis (IA) based on the presence of any of the following: **peri-transplant renal replacement therapy (RRT)**, **re-transplantation**, or **transplantation for fulminant hepatic failure**.

bedside_protocol.title: Initiate Targeted Prophylaxis for High-Risk Patients
bedside_protocol.action: For high-risk liver transplant recipients, start anti-Aspergillus prophylaxis with a preferred agent: an **echinocandin (e.g., micafungin, anidulafungin, caspofungin)** or a **newer mold-active triazole (voriconazole, posaconazole, or isavuconazole)**.

bedside_protocol.title: Determine Prophylaxis Duration
bedside_protocol.action: Continue prophylaxis **until discharge from the initial transplant hospitalization or for a maximum of 28 days**, whichever occurs first. Reassess need if new risk factors (e.g., RRT, reoperation) emerge later.

bedside_protocol.title: Monitor for Drug Interactions and Toxicity
bedside_protocol.action: If using a mold-active triazole, perform **therapeutic drug monitoring of calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus, cyclosporine)** and monitor for hepatotoxicity and QTc prolongation. If using an echinocandin, monitor for breakthrough *Candida* infections.

bedside_protocol.title: Manage Kidney and Pancreas Transplant Recipients
bedside_protocol.action: Universal anti-Aspergillus prophylaxis is **not recommended**. Standard peri-operative yeast prophylaxis (e.g., fluconazole or nystatin) may be used per local protocols.

strengths_limitations:
- Strengths: Systematic review and meta-analysis using GRADE methodology; clear, actionable recommendations based on pre-defined decision thresholds; comprehensive mapping review to establish baseline IA incidence.
- Limitations: Evidence is largely from older studies with heterogeneous agents and designs; very low to low certainty of evidence for most recommendations; lack of direct comparative data for newer triazoles in liver transplant recipients; kidney and pancreas sections are based on observational data without formal recommendations.

drugs_doses.dose: Not specified; duration until hospital discharge or up to 28 days
drugs_doses.indication: Targeted anti-Aspergillus prophylaxis in high-risk liver transplant recipients
drugs_doses.adverse_effects: Hepatotoxicity (2.1%), QTc prolongation, visual disturbances, skin reactions/photocarcinogenesis, significant CYP3A4 drug interactions with calcineurin inhibitors

drugs_doses.dose: Not specified; duration until hospital discharge or up to 28 days
drugs_doses.indication: Targeted anti-Aspergillus prophylaxis in high-risk liver transplant recipients (extrapolated evidence)
drugs_doses.adverse_effects: Hepatotoxicity, QTc prolongation, GI symptoms, significant CYP3A4 drug interactions; limited data in liver transplant

drugs_doses.dose: Not specified; duration until hospital discharge or up to 28 days
drugs_doses.indication: Targeted anti-Aspergillus prophylaxis in high-risk liver transplant recipients (extrapolated evidence)
drugs_doses.adverse_effects: Mild peripheral edema (1%), hepatotoxicity; limited data in liver transplant; CYP3A4 interactions

drugs_doses.dose: Not specified; duration up to 21 days or until hospital discharge
drugs_doses.indication: Targeted anti-Aspergillus prophylaxis in high-risk liver transplant recipients
drugs_doses.adverse_effects: Well tolerated; 1% discontinuation for QTc prolongation; breakthrough IA 0-3%; no activity against Cryptococcus or Rhizopus

drugs_doses.dose: Not specified; duration up to 21 days or until hospital discharge
drugs_doses.indication: Targeted anti-Aspergillus prophylaxis in high-risk liver transplant recipients
drugs_doses.adverse_effects: Low rates of hepatic/renal events; breakthrough IA up to 11% in one high-risk cohort; breakthrough Candida parapsilosis reported

drugs_doses.dose: Not specified; duration until hospital discharge
drugs_doses.indication: Targeted anti-Aspergillus prophylaxis in high-risk liver transplant recipients
drugs_doses.adverse_effects: Grade IV lab abnormalities ~30% (attributed to post-transplant changes); 8% discontinuation; breakthrough IA 0-1%

drugs_doses.dose: Not specified; duration 14 days to until hospital discharge
drugs_doses.indication: Alternative for targeted anti-Aspergillus prophylaxis when triazoles/echinocandins are contraindicated
drugs_doses.adverse_effects: Nephrotoxicity (2.5-7.8%), infusion reactions, electrolyte disturbances; SAEs leading to discontinuation in 5-13%

drugs_doses.dose: Not specified; duration up to 10 weeks
drugs_doses.indication: Historical agent; now infrequently used for prophylaxis
drugs_doses.adverse_effects: Non-serious AEs in 24.3-51% (GI intolerance); unreliable oral absorption; significant CYP3A4 interactions; hepatotoxicity
